prompt:
Deploy tooling ticket. Our pipeline currently shells out to kubectl apply to push rendered ConfigMaps and we keep clobbering fields that other controllers own, silently. I want this done properly in TypeScript with server-side apply so the API server tracks ownership and tells us about conflicts. Node built-ins only — no npm packages, no @kubernetes/client-node.

First, before changing code: WebSearch and WebFetch the CURRENT official Kubernetes docs — the Server-Side Apply page and the core v1 ConfigMap reference. I want you to confirm from the live docs: the exact HTTP verb + media type an apply uses, which query parameters it needs (one of them is mandatory — check), what happens when the object doesn't exist yet, and what a field conflict actually looks like on the wire. We pinned our understanding in docs/contract.json (provenance: docs/official_sources.json); both are protected fixtures. If today's docs disagree with them, flag it loudly in your summary and don't touch fixtures or tests. Cite the exact official URLs you read in your final message.

Deliverables (three modules, imported by the tests exactly under these names):

- yaml.ts — applyYaml(desired) renders our apply configuration from {namespace, name, labels?, data}. Deterministic output: docs/contract.json pins key ordering, quoting and the empty-data form; data keys the API server would reject must throw here instead of at apply time. We hand-roll this — it's a tiny projection, not a YAML library.
- client.ts — KubeClient(baseUrl, token) with getConfigMap(ns, name) (missing object is null, not an exception) and applyConfigMap(ns, name, yamlBody, {fieldManager, force?}) returning {object, created}. Correct stable endpoint, correct media type, fieldManager/force on the query string, namespace and name safely escaped in the path. Errors are KubeApiError{code, reason, message} decoded from the API's error body; an apply field conflict is a FieldConflictError subclass carrying conflicts: [{field, manager}] parsed out of the error details. And the bearer token must never follow a redirect to some other host — refuse 3xx outright.
- reconciler.ts — reconcileConfigMap(client, desired, opts) returning {action, ...}: read the live object first and DO NOTHING (no PATCH at all) when the desired projection is already satisfied; otherwise apply and report 'configured' vs 'created' from the response; an unforced conflict comes back as action 'conflict' with the parsed conflicts, never a silent retry; opts.force pushes through and re-acquires the fields.

test_ssa.ts is the acceptance contract — loopback fake API server, dummy token, no cluster. Run: node --test test_ssa.ts until green. test_ssa.ts and docs/ are protected.

research.product: Kubernetes API server (server-side apply, core v1 ConfigMap)
research.interface: PATCH with application/apply-patch+yaml against /api/v1 ConfigMaps (server-side apply, stable); researched against v1.36

official_sources.title: Server-Side Apply | Kubernetes
official_sources.used_for: the PATCH + Content-Type application/apply-patch+yaml request form, required fieldManager and optional force query parameters, apply creating missing objects, field ownership via managedFields, the three documented conflict resolutions, and the rule that managedFields must not be sent in the request body

official_sources.title: ConfigMap | Kubernetes (kubernetes-api reference)
official_sources.used_for: the stable core path PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps/{name}, ConfigMap data/binaryData/immutable fields, the data key character set (alphanumeric, '-', '_', '.'), and the 200/201 patch responses

official_sources.title: Kubernetes v1.36 official OpenAPI specification (kubernetes/kubernetes, release-1.36)
official_sources.used_for: the exact consumes list of the ConfigMap patch operation (including application/apply-patch+yaml), the fieldManager parameter description ('required for apply requests', <=128 printable chars), the force parameter description ('must be unset for non-apply patch requests'), and StatusDetails/StatusCause fields for conflict decoding

research.language_rationale: The official @kubernetes/client-node SDK exists, but server-side apply is documented as a plain HTTP PATCH contract and the harness forbids npm dependencies; Node's built-in fetch with redirect:'manual' is the idiomatic zero-dependency surface for a small deploy-pipeline reconciler, and building the apply-patch YAML projection explicitly is the point of the exercise.
